text:p.text:span: Speak to Puzur-Numuåda, governor of Kazallu,
saying (the words of) Ibbi-Sin, your king:

office:text.text:p:
4 Ever since I selected you out of the ranks and made you the governor of Kazallu, 5 have you not been honored among the troops as my representative? 6 How could you send someone to me (with a letter beginning) thus:1
Var.: But you sent me someone with the following message. 7 “Iåbi-Erra has presented his matter before me,” ­8 (and ending with) “and as far as I am concerned, when­ he finally strikes, I will have to flee!” 9 How is it that you do not know how to repulse Iåbi-Erra­ (back) to his country? 10–11 Why did you and Girbubu, ­governor of Ğirkal, not confront him with the troops that are under your authority (while you still had the chance)? 12 To restore the lands to their previous condition—that was the assignment you were both given!

text:p.text:span: At some time in the past, Enlil had already come to hate Sumer,
and had elevated a monkey descending from his mountain (home) to the stewardship of the homeland.
But now Enlil has given the kingship to a peddler of
, to Iåbi-Erra, ­who is not even of Sumerian seed.
Moreover, the assembly of­ the gods has scattered Sumer.
“As long as an enemy is installed in Ur,
Iåbi-Erra, the man of Mari, will continue to rip out its­ foundations,
and so Sumer will be measured out (like grain)”—thus he spoke!
But if you both gather together the governors of all the individual cities,
it is by the (very) command of Enlil that they will ­overthrow Iåbi-Erra!
But even if you should hand over the city to the enemy as an ally, do you think that Iåbi-Erra would not recognize that you are my trusty servant?
and bring an end to all treason
and have your men work on their harvest.
But neither of you should do the reaping yourself nor run away to me;
I assure you that he will not conquer (your) ­city;
the man from Mari, with the mind of a beast,
For even now Enlil has roused up the Amorites from the highlands to aid me.
They will repulse the Elamite (forces) and capture Iåbi-Erra
to restore the homeland, so that all the lands will know the power!
Please, you both must not neglect (my orders)!
This text has a double line after line 35 and therefore definitely had the short version.
X2 = MM 1039 (M. Molina and B. Böck

office:text.text:p: X2 collated on photographs provided by Miguel Civil and Manuel Molina.
X1 possibly from Sippar, X2 reportedly from Babylon. N1 and N2 were written by the same scribe.
Type 1: N4, X1; all others type III.
N3 oo o oo o o o o o . o

text:p.text:span: N3 o o o o o o o o o o . . . .

office:text.text:p: Si1 o . . . o . o o o + . o o o o o o o
N3 o o o o o o . . + + + +
N4 . o o o o o o o o o o o

text:p.text:span: Si1 o o . . kalam-ma . o o o o o

office:text.text:p: N3 o o o o o o o . + . . +
N4 . o o o o o o o o o o o
Si1 . . + + + . o o o o o o
N3 oo o oo o o . + . . . + + + +

text:p.text:span: + . o o o o o o o o

office:text.text:p: N3 o o o o o o o . . .
X2 o o o o o o o o x +
9. en-na iš-bi-dèr-ra kur-šè bí-in-gi4-a a-gin7 nu-e-zu

text:p.text:span: N3 o o o o oo o . – šu-

text:note-body.text:p: It is possible that the last two words were either wrapped around and broken, or may have been at the beginning of ine 10.

text:p.text:span: . + -+ . o o o o o o o o o o o
X2 o o o o oo . kur ki-bi-
N3 o o o o o . + . . + +
Ki1 o o o o o o . . . - .
Si1 + + + + . o o o o o o
X2 o o o o o . + + + + +
N3 o o o o o o o o o . . . . o . . . o o o o

Si + + + + + + . o o o o o o o + . o o o o o

text:p.text:span: X2 o o o o o o o in-gál-le-eš + + + + + + ­su
N3 o o o o o o o . . .

office:text.text:p: Si1 . . . o o o o o o o o o o o o
N3 o o o oo o o o + + + . . o
N o o o oo o x . o o o o o o
X2 o o o oo oo o o o o x + ba-an-gig

text:p.text:span: N3 o o o o o . + + + + . o o o o o

office:text.text:p: N4 A.UGU+ + . + + o o + + + + o o o o
Ki1 o o o o o o o o + + . + + + . o

text:p.text:span: X2 o o o o o o .

office:text.text:p: N3 o o o oo o + + + + + o o o o
Ki1 o o o oo o o o o . . + ha la +
X2 o o o oo o o o o o o nu ha ra

text:p.text:span: N4 ++ + + + . o o o o o o o o . + . o o o o

office:text.text:p: Ki1 oo o o o o o o o o o . . . . . o o o o o

text:p.text:span: X2 o o o o o o o o o o o o x . ù-bí-in-
N4 + + ++ + . . o o o dib + + aš

office:text.text:p:
N1 + + + + . + + + +5
Proceeded by an erased árad. + + +
N3 + + + . o o oo o o o . + +

text:p.text:span: X2 o o o o o o oo .

office:text.text:p: N3 + + + . o o o o o o . +

X1 o o o . . + šè ba-ra-mu-un-

office:text.text:p: X2 o o o o o . ma ba-ra-an-šúm-ma-a
X2 o o o o o o o o o o . + ní-bi mu-un-zu-a
N4 . o o o o o . + + + +
N4 o o o o o + + -
N4 o o o o o o o . + + + + + .
N4 o o o o o o o + + + x
Si1 o . . o o o o o o o
N4 o o o o o o . + - + na . . . . .
Si1 o + + + x o o o o o o o o o o

office:text.text:p: Si1 + + + . + + o o oo o o o + o o o
N4 o o o o o o . . . o o o . . .
Si1 + + + + + da o o o o o o o o o :

text:note-body.text:p: The equivalents of the beginning parts of the line may have been on line 34.

text:p.text:span: X1 with variants from X2 (equivalent lines in A in right-hand column)
X1 has deteriorated somewhat sine I first began to study it; see the comments on p.

There seem to be remnants of the last sign of at least three lines before this, as well as the additional line that does not appear to match X1. Unfortunately, I have not been able to inspect this tablet in person.
22.ga-nam pu-úh-rum dingir-re-e-ne ki-en-gi bir-bir-re ba-[… d]ug
Speak to Puzur-Åulgi, governor of Kazallu,

office:text.text:p:
3 Ever since I selected you out of the ranks and made you the governor of Kazallu, 4have you not been honored throughout the homeland as my representative? 5 Kazallu … 6 A man who is …7 Enlil has not …from your frontier area. 8 Someone excellent, a man who is like you … ­the responsibilities over the smaller cities … 9 Why is it that all valor has been torn from your heart, 10 why are you so panicked and full of contempt? 11 You even sent someone to me (saying) thus:10
Var.: But you sent me someone with the following message. 12 “Iåbi-Erra has presented his matter before me, 13 and as far as I am concerned, when­ he finally strikes, I will have to flee!” 14 How is it that you do not know how to repulse Iåbi-Erra­ to his own country? 15–16 Why did you and Ğirbubu, governor of Ğirkal, not stand up against him with the troops ­that are under your authority (while you still had the chance)? 17 To restore the lands to their previous condition—that was the assignment you were both given!

text:p.text:span: Enlil had earlier already come to hate Sumer,
appointing a monkey descending from its mountain (home) to the stewardship of the homeland.
But now Enlil has handed ­kingship to a (mere)
, one who chases the wind, to Iåbi-Erra, who is not even of­ Sumerian descent.
Moreover, once the assembly of the gods, (decided) to scatter the (inhabitants of the) Sumer,
Father Enlil, having conveyed his commands, proceeded to overthrow the homeland.
Iåbi-Erra, the man of Mari, will tear out its foundations,
And if the governors of the individual cities should gather together,
by Enlil’s command Iåbi-Erra will fall.
But even supposing that he is not (betrayed) as if being handed over to an enemy city by a comrade,
and you, although acting as if you were my trusted servant, recognize Iåbi-Erra by yourself/out of fear,
your statements would do nothing to upset me,
for you know very well that I am (a favorite) of the gods.
Father Enlil, by means of his angry commands, has overthrown the homeland.
Come now, he has returned to my side!
My complaints were submitted by humble prayer
and Great Lord Enlil heard me out;
he cast his favorable glance upon me,
set his holy heart on­ mercy,
and established for me my favorable omen. ­
And after I had the follow-up reading made concerning his
So X1; X2 omits 41 and 42: After I had the follow-up reading made concerning the left and right sides, (the message was): …
the weapon-mark on my right side, its trunk is straight, and that (good news) gave me joy;
on the weapon-mark on his (i.e., Iåbi-Erra’s) left side a filament is suspended,
it is placed (against) the other side.
(The message was:) “My enemy ­shall fall into my hands, he shall be killed,
the people, having come out of darkness into the light, will lie in
Utu, lord who makes the decisions of the heavens­ and the earth, has provided (this) omen.
come now, ­(you too) must return to reconciliation,
so that all treachery is undone
so that ­your can people can work on their harvest.
You must not hurry to come to me;
(I assure you that) he will not conquer the city that he (wants),
the ­man of Mari, with the mind of a beast, will not exercise sovereignty there!
Now Enlil has mustered the Tidnumite(s) in (the ­land of) Amurrum as my helper(s).
the homeland­ will be restored (to me).
It is urgent, you must make sure that everyone must not neglect (my orders)!

This letter is known in two versions, a shorter one (A) from Nippur, Kish, and Sippar, and a longer one (B) attested in two manuscripts of unknown provenience. As noted above, there are clues that suggest that X1 may come from Sippar and X2 from Babylon but the evidence is hardly conclusive. Version B adds six lines between lines 5 and 6, fourteen or fifteen lines between 25 and 26, and two lines between 32 and 33. The short version appears to be fairly stable, with few substantive variants but the two sources of B clearly represent different redactional traditions. X1 is dated to the 27th year of Samsu-iluna, providing a historical context for one manuscript the long version. Moreover, the text was known to the compiler of the
, as documented by the entry in line 8:

The differences between the two versions are not only in length. A has many difficult passages, and some of them seem to have been as puzzling to some ancients as they are to many of us. Redactors of what became “B” — itself not uniform—attempted to make sense of some version of “A” that was at their disposal, reinterpreting some of the grammar and vocabulary to fit a specific understanding of the text.

office:text.text:p: B10. This difficult line, which is not in the short version, contains words that are otherwise rare in Sumerian. The noun ní-ul4, “fear, terror,” occurs, to my knowledge, only once in another text, in Ininåagura 161 (see Å. Sjöberg, ZA 65 [1975] 161): ní-àm ur4-re ní ≠ul4 ní-≠ri-ti-la ní gal me-lám-ma, which consists of a series of synonyms and quasi-synonyms for “fear,” “terror.” Here ul4 seems to qualify the noun in the compound verb ní…te, “to fear;” it could either be construed as ul4 = piqittu, “terror,” or as the adverb “greatly” (magal); see now M. Jaques, Le vocabulaire des sentiments 189.

text:p.text:span: , “to hold in contempt, despise,” which is otherwise only attested in
16 and in bilingual and lexical texts (=
is used, like its Akkadian equivalent
, to introduce direct speech. In line 22 it follows the quotation, and the form is
, which is likewise used to introduce quoted statements.
The verbal form in A is, technically speaking, in the third person, “he sent a man to you,” but that is clearly incorrect. A redactor of B attempted to clarify this with the superfluous
. These are the incipit and last lines of the previous­ letter; this is a unique example of the way in which one could ­refer to previous correspondence in Sumerian.
. The syntax of this line is difficult. The first part, governed by a temporal adverb (
), must be dependent on the main clause, which is introduced by an interrogative preposition (
). Redactors of the long version recognized the problem and attempted to resolve it by changing the predicate of the dependent clause but only managed to introduce more confusion.
. The proper understanding of this passage relies on the first word, which is
in one manuscript of A but clarified as
The scribe of X1 has taken pains to interpret a difficult passage, since in all other cases
); here it must be a temporal adverb. These lines have to be compared to
98–100 with commentary on p. 159; H. Vanstiphout,

office:text.text:p: Earlier, whenever the bird screeched at its nest,
Its young answered it from its nest,
But now, when the bird screeched at its nest,

text:p.text:span: Its young did not answer it from its nest.
Thus, the phrases governed by the adverbs

(B) are structurally identical, contrasting earlier and later events. This is extremely important; we now realize that the word “monkey” does not refer to Iåbi-Erra, as has always been thought but must be a characterization of someone else, undoubtedly “Elamites.”
Is it possible that this refers to events that provided the enigmatic name of Ibbi-Sin’s 23rd, and penultimate, year:
from its mountain against Ibbi-Sin, king of Ur.” Note that the Gutians are described as having the form of monkeys (
, and the same expression is used to characterize Amorites in the

(line 127). This may be a reference to some other highlanders here, Elamites, or perhaps the Amorites, according to IbIå1A: 14’ (letter 22), although the latter is definitely untrustworthy. More important, if this holds true, it would date the Puzur-Numuåda correspondence to the very end of Ibbi-Sin’s reign. See pp.

is usually translated as “to hate.” The sense here is more precise: Enlil has broken his bond with Sumer, and thus the verb is used here in the “judicial” sense of scorn or repudiation discussed in M. Jaques,
, which is­ attested in at least five manuscripts of the short version, is ­grammatically and lexically difficult. The term
, has been rendered as Asafoedita, ever since it was­ thus defined in R. C. Thompson,

179 n. 1, with earlier references to ­our passage. As far as I can gather, there is not a single other example of this word in any Sumerian text outside of the lexical tradition (documented in the dictionaries). It is listed in an OB school coefficient list of unknown origin, followed by

is a foul­ smelling spice, and thus peddlers of such a substance would have been ­tainted by its odor. But there is no evidence to support this­ identification, etymology aside (“unclean”), as the extant documentation provides no­ evidence on the nature of this plant.
T 400: “The identification with asafoedita in Thompson DAB 358 is not supported by the botanical evidence.”
as anyone who has cooked northern Indian food knows
“is ­virtually odorless until it is powdered, when it releases its ­strong characteristic smell” (J. Sahni,
9). It was a foreign plant, at least in Assyria, since it was listed as such by Assurnaœirpal in his “Banquet Stela” (
66, claims that Asafoedita was grown in the gardens of Merodach-Baladan (
14 50:65) but the plant in question is
, a different, albeit unidentified plant. The identification of the plant as Asafoedita was already questioned by A. Kilmer,
Perhaps this is the clue to the metaphor: Iåbi-Erra is a mere peddler of exotic foreign substances, once again stressing his foreign origins.

office:text.text:p: The two versions have different forms of the line:

text:p.text:span: (At present the end of the line in X1 reads:
The text was in better condition when it was cited by­ B. Landsberger,
179 n. 1. X2 has the idiosyncratic syllabic writing
. The end of the line may have to be read as
is unique. Compare, perhaps, the invective raised against Åamåi-Addu by­ Puzur-Sin of Assur:
, “not of the flesh of the city­ of Assur” (A. K. Grayson,
3 [1985] 12 lines 12–13 and 25; see already J. Cooper,
seems to be used in two different ways, either as an adverb meaning “moreover,” (Akk.
), or as a particle introducing irrealis (
27 [1968] 240–42). Note that the irrealis occurrences, as well as all Akkadian translations, are post-OB and have no relevance for this passage.
but in a sense combined the two, apparently creating a neologism, a compound verb
Œ 25). It may not have been properly understood by the author of B.

office:text.text:p: A19 = B24. The final sign in lú-NE.RU-åa (only in A; B: -ra) presents a problem. This­ passage was discussed by Å. Sjöberg, ZA 83 (1993) 18–19. Most of ­the evidence points to a reading érim of NE.RU but this clearly demonstrates that some scribes read the signs NE.RU.DU as ne-ru-åa4. ­

text:p.text:span: . The interpretation of A offered here is hardly secure, as the line has no known parallels, and the syntax is difficult to analyze. These problems may have already vexed the ancients; somewhere along the line of transmission someone either did not understand the passage correctly, or wanted to amend it to reflect better a particular understanding of the lines. This may have been a multi-step process. In A25 the verbal form
is positive but in B29 the two sources differ. X1 has the negative
, which may be either negative or positive second person. This change is echoed in the verb in the next line, where negative prefix
, “fear,” or as the reflexive particle, and the verbal form changed to positive. The new interpretation may have then led to the insertion of the two new lines that follow in B but were not in A.
From a syntactic point of view, these lines are difficult to analyze; at first glance we have two subordinate clauses with no main sentence. With some reservations, I take
not as a subordinated verbal form but as a question, although one would expect
. The basic meaning of the passage is: even if you were to surrender to him, do you think he would really believe that you were no longer loyal to me?
. The first verb is different in all manuscripts. The composite text follows N1 (
. The tradition of X1 (B) once again follows a track of reinterpretation. The root
was found wanting and was understood as
, “to hurry;” under Akkadian influence the two verbs in the line were taken as hendiadys.
, “to conquer,” discussed in the commentary to PuIb1: 16 (23). Indeed, this line undoubtedly alludes to that particular line in the letter which this one answers. The use of the unseparated
, used in all three Nippur sources,
is characteristic of Larsa Sumerian but is unique in this letter, in which compound verbs are otherwise are properly used correctly. Note that the author of X1 insisted on a correct form (
. The complex ideological games that are being played here are echoed in the OB omen tradition, which preserves
a very different version of these events, using very similar language, albeit in Akkadian (
, “If there is a large Weapon-mark present in the pars familiaris, and it straddles the gall-bladder, it is the Weapon-mark of Iåbi-Erra, who repulsed the Elamite (forces).” Note that Akkadian
is the lexical equivalent of Sumerian
, “to push away, to repulse.” In both versions of the letter the promise is that it will be Ibbi-Sin who will repulse Elam, and capture Iåbi-Erra.
. Note the use of the uncommon
. For examples of the former, which is often misinterpreted, see F. Karahashi,
. The normal Sumerian version of this expression does not have
. The only other example that is similar to the one found here known to me is found in an incantation
utu agrun-na-ta [x x x] igi ní
-ADJ in Sumerian is not clear to me.

. This is the only actual omen in Sumerian and­ therefore requires a full discussion. The technical vocabulary­ is unique; it must be compared to the Akkadian language of­ extispicy, since there are no second-millennium omens in Sumerian. The passage in question is attested only in non-Nippurean manuscripts and was clearly invented by some northern scribe, who cleverly invented a Sumerian technical vocabulary for extispicy. The observations in Michalowski,
247–58 must now be adjusted accordingly. Nevertheless, the new rendition of these lines remains hypothetical, at best.
. This line contains two technical terms:
is sometimes used as­ synonym for
but here it probably has a more limited­ meaning; the phrase is a back translation from Akkadian
probably reflects the more generalized technical use of
various sorts of ominous phenomena, as discussed by J.-P. Durand,
. In X1, the two sides of the liver are here described as
15–29. In X2 there are only the two sides, without

; this scribe also omits lines 41 and 42, that is the laborious Sumerian description of the exta. Without additional duplicates it is impossible to decide if X2 actually omits these lines, or if they were added by the scribe of X1.
as “superior strength,” and this is clearly not at issue here. There remains the administrative term

, “additional work (assignment),” listed in the dictionary on the following page, and one might consider that the usage in the letter is related to the latter. I see three possibilities here. Either the author used it here in an adverbial sense, and the hence the translation should be “

this is another attempt to create a Sumerian extispicy term in Sumerian. The two texts differ substantially here but note that the technically incorrect ascription of the right side to Iåbi-Erra and the left to Ibbi-Sin in X1 in line 40 seems to be reversed in 41 and 42. Therefore, with all due caution, I suggest that
is an attempt to render Akkadian
, “follow-up.” As I understand it, Ibbi-Sin implies, according to the Old Babylonian author, that he had omens performed, and the follow-up was favorable to him, as he relates here.
, “Weapon of the right/left (side of a permanent feature);
For the Akkadian see U. Jeyes,
has no parts in Akkadian extispicy; Ulla Koch suggests to me that
may therefore refer to something else, perhaps a part of the gall bladder.
, “filament,” is another negative mark; see U. Jeyes,
63 with previous literature. As Jeyes observed, this feature connotes restraint or obstruction, which in this case must pertain to Iåbi-Erra. The verb
, “to be suspended, dangle,” or perhaps even
, “to attach” (suggested to me by R. D. Biggs), both of which are commonly used in conjunction with the “filament.”
. Note that the apodosis of this omen­ contains a Sumerian calque of an Akkadian expression that is­ found in omen and dream reports from Mari;
here used in­ the sense of

,­ “I will deliver your enemies to you,” (quotation from the speech­ of a woman who fell in a trance in the temple of Annunitum). ­Similar phrases are found in the highly formalized letters of Dam-æuraœim to Zimri-Lim,
. The motif of turning darkness into light, or the ­reverse, a metaphor for the reversal of fortune, is ­well attested in Sumerian literature; see, e.g.,

, “In the­ city where bright sunlight used to shine forth, the day darkened. ­In Eridu, where bright sunlight used to shine forth, the day ­darkened. As if the sun had set below the horizon, it turned­ into twilight”; see also
already in Ur III; note the playful writing
for the place name Huhnuri in B. Lafont,­
79:13. More to the point is the similarity with expressions found in OB “historical” omens pertaining to Sargon and the otherwise unknown TE-Enlila:
, “Omen of Sargon who went through/made an incursion into the darkness and then saw/had appear light;”
, “omen of TE-Enlil for whom the light appeared;” for references see A. Goetze,
The end of the line is grammatically difficult. One could read
. The two versions have very different interpretations of this line;
, “peace, reconciliation,” known only from bilingual texts and as a logogram in Akkadian, rather than the more common “favorable pronouncement.”
in B is very rare; it is found only in inscriptions of Warad-Sin and Rim-Sin in the expression
, which seems to mean “more than previously/before.”
is unique; it ­must be compared with the line in
as “Bitte ich kann nicht zulassen, dass du immer wieder unzuverlässig und falsch bist” (
25 [1973] 116, with commentary on p. 124). My interpretation is based on
, “to undo.” The author of B had trouble with this line may have interpreted
. The translation of this line has been taken for granted but it is not without problems. Were it not for similar sentiments found in other texts, one would interpret
, the form found in all the Nippur manuscripts, as “of hostile intentions,” rather than as “the mind of a dog/beast.” For
, “Gutium, the enemy, overturned….” “Mind of a beast” would be
, and, indeed, the former is what X1 writes. The most instructive parallel is in
, “With human instinct but the mind of a dog/beast, and monkey’s looks” (see J. Cooper,

31, 353 n. 46, with parallels). It is probable that the Nippur scribes understood this passage as referring to “hostile intentions,” but that the ever-vigilant author of X1 hyper-corrected it to conform to the usage he knew from other texts.

. From the purely formal grammatical point of view the Amorites are referred to in singular third person of the animate class. The tradition represented by X1 (B) indicates that someone recognized this and attempted to take it literally, hence the otherwise inexplicable writing
, which I take to be a clumsy attempt at a Sumerian
. Note that only manuscript Si1 among the A version texts has
. Following the singular second person pronoun

, most manuscripts have a different form of the final verb: two have second persona plural (N2, N3), one has first/second singular (N1 but this might be simply abbreviation of the plural form), one has third plural (X1, probably also Si1). Most probably, the confusion derives from the contrast between the singular independent pronoun, addressed to Puzur-Numuåda, and the plural verb form that references both him and
, “my king, (the troops) will not neglect (your orders).”
